perturbation.original_text: The Recipient shall keep the Disclosing Party's Confidential Information confidential and, except with the prior written consent of the Disclosing Party, shall: (a) not use or exploit the Confidential Information in any way except for the P urpose; (b) not disclose or make available the Confidential Information in whole or in part to any third party, except as expressly permitted by this Agreement; (c) not copy, reduce to writing or otherwise record the Confidential Information except as strictly n ecessary for the Purpose (and any such copies, reductions to writing and records shall be the property of the Disclosing Party); (d) keep separate the Confidential Information from all documents and other records of the Recipient; (e) apply the same security meas ures and degree of care to the Confidential Information as the Recipient applies to its own confidential information, which the Recipient warrants as providing adequate protection from unauthori zed disclosure, copying or use; and (f) keep a written record of: any document or other Confidential Information received from the other in tangible form; any copy made of the Confidential Information.
perturbation.changed_text: The Recipient shall endeavor to keep the Disclosing Party's Confidential Information confidential and, except with the prior written consent of the Disclosing Party, shall: (a) not use or exploit the Confidential Information in any way except for the P urpose; (b) not disclose or make available the Confidential Information in whole or in part to any third party, except as expressly permitted by this Agreement; (c) not copy, reduce to writing or otherwise record the Confidential Information except when necessary for the Purpose (and any such copies, reductions to writing and records shall be the property of the Disclosing Party); (d) keep separate the Confidential Information from all documents and other records of the Recipient; (e) apply security measures and a degree of care to the Confidential Information; and (f) maintain a record of: any document or other Confidential Information received from the other in tangible form; any copy made of the Confidential Information.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'shall keep' to 'shall endeavor to keep' introduces ambiguity and weakens the obligation. Replacing 'as strictly necessary' with 'when necessary' makes the requirement less stringent. Removing the warranty about 'adequate protection' weakens the responsibility. Changing 'keep a written record' to 'maintain a record' also weakens it. These modifications make enforcement more difficult.
perturbation.law_explanation: DUTSA requires reasonable measures to protect trade secrets. The changes weaken the obligation to protect confidential information, making it potentially non-compliant with the required 'reasonable measures' under Delaware law for trade secret protection. Specifically, the 'endeavor to keep' wording makes the protection non-absolute, and the removal of 'adequate protection' warranty weakens the standard of care.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Delaware Code Online Title 6 Authenticated PDF Â§ 2001 Â§ 2002 Â§ 2003 Â§ 2004 Â§ 2005 Â§ 2006 Â§ 2007 Â§ 2008 Â§ 2009 TITLE 6 Commerce and Trade SUBTITLE II Other Laws Relating to Commerce and Trade CHAPTER 20. Trade Secrets Â§ 2001. Definitions. The following words, terms and phrases, when used in this chapter, shall have the meanings ascribed to them in this section, except where the context clearly indicates a different meaning: (1) âImproper meansâ shall include theft, bribery, misrepresentation, breach or inducement of a breach of a duty to maintain secrecy, or espionage through electronic or other means. (2) âMisappropriationâ shall mean: a. Acquisition of a trade secret of another by a person who knows or has reason to know that the trade secret was acquired by improper means; or b. Disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent by a person who: 1. Used improper means to acquire knowledge of the trade secret; or 2. At the time of disclosure or use, knew or had reason to know that his or her knowledge of the trade was: A. Derived from or through a person who had utilized improper means to acquire it; B. Acquired under circumstances giving rise to a duty to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or C. Derived from or through a person who owed a duty to the person seeking relief to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or 3. Before a material change of the personâs position, knew or had reason to know that it was a trade secret and that knowledge of it had been acquired by accident or mistake. (3) âPersonâ shall mean a natural person, corporation, statutory trust, business trust, estate, trust, partnership, association, joint venture, government, governmental subdivision or agency, or any other legal or commercial entity. (4) âTrade secretâ shall mean information, including a formula, pattern, compilation, program, device, method, technique or process, that: a. Derives independent economic value, actual or potential, from not being generally known to, and not being readily ascertainab

perturbation.justification: The modifications weaken the obligations to protect confidential information, potentially conflicting with DUTSA's requirement for 'reasonable measures' under Delaware law. The original text uses 'shall keep,' which is a direct obligation. Changing it to 'shall endeavor to keep' introduces ambiguity and a lower standard of care. The removal of the warranty regarding 'adequate protection' further weakens this standard. Scraped Law Snippet 1 defines 'Misappropriation' as the 'Acquisition of a trade secret of another by a person who knows or has reason to know that the trade secret was acquired by improper means' or 'Disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent.' By weakening the obligation to protect confidential information, the modified contract increases the risk of misappropriation, thereby creating a contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: 2.5 The Recipient shall establish and maintain adequate security measures (including any reasonable security measures proposed by the Disclosing party from time to time) to safeguard the Confidential Information from unauthori zed access or use.
perturbation.changed_text: 2.5 The Recipient shall consider establishing security measures to safeguard the Confidential Information from unauthorized access or use.
perturbation.explanation: Replacing 'shall establish and maintain adequate security measures' with 'shall consider establishing security measures' significantly weakens the obligation. It makes implementing security measures discretionary rather than mandatory, creating ambiguity regarding the level of protection required.
perturbation.law_explanation: DUTSA requires 'reasonable efforts' to maintain the secrecy of trade secrets. By making the implementation of security measures discretionary, the modified text may not meet the 'reasonable efforts' standard required to protect confidential information, thereby risking the loss of trade secret protection under Delaware law.

perturbation.justification: The original contract language mandates the Recipient to 'establish and maintain adequate security measures,' creating a clear obligation to protect confidential information. The changed text, however, replaces this with 'shall consider establishing security measures,' making the implementation of such measures discretionary. This directly weakens the obligation to protect confidential information, which is critical under trade secret law. According to the Law Explanation, DUTSA requires 'reasonable efforts' to maintain the secrecy of trade secrets, and the change reduces the likelihood of meeting that standard, risking the loss of trade secret protection. The scraped snippets, particularly Snippet 1 which defines 'Misappropriation' and 'Improper means', implies a duty to maintain secrecy. By making security measures discretionary, the changed text increases the risk of misappropriation through 'improper means' such as 'breach of a duty to maintain secrecy', thus creating a potential legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: 3.1 At the request of the Disclosing Party, the Recipient shall: (a) destroy or return to the Disclos ing Party all documents and materials (and any copies) containing, reflecting, incorporating, or based on the Disclosing Party's Confidential Information; (b) save in relation to its backup email systems, erase all the Disclosing Party's Confidential Informati on from its computer systems or which is stored in electronic form ; and (c) certify in writing to the Disclosing Party that it has complied with the requirements of this Clause, provided that a Recipient may retain documents and materials containing, reflectin g, incorporating, or based on the Disclosing Party's Confidential Information to the extent required by law or any applicable governmental or regulatory authority and to the extent reasonable to permit the Recipient to keep evidence that it has performed i ts obligations under this Agreement. The provisions of this Agreement shall continue to apply to any documents and materials retained by the Recipient.
perturbation.changed_text: 3.1 At the request of the Disclosing Party, the Recipient should consider destroying or returning to the Disclos ing Party all documents and materials (and any copies) containing, reflecting, incorporating, or based on the Disclosing Party's Confidential Information; (b) erase all the Disclosing Party's Confidential Informati on from its computer systems or which is stored in electronic form to a reasonable extent; and (c) provide some form of confirmation to the Disclosing Party that it has attempted to comply with the requirements of this Clause, provided that a Recipient may retain documents and materials containing, reflectin g, incorporating, or based on the Disclosing Party's Confidential Information to the extent required by law or any applicable governmental or regulatory authority and to the extent reasonable to permit the Recipient to keep evidence that it has performed i ts obligations under this Agreement. The provisions of this Agreement shall continue to apply to any documents and materials retained by the Recipient.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'shall' to 'should consider' makes the return or destruction of confidential information optional. Adding 'to a reasonable extent' introduces ambiguity regarding the completeness of data erasure. Replacing 'certify in writing' with 'provide some form of confirmation' weakens the verification process. These modifications create uncertainty about the recipient's obligations.
perturbation.law_explanation: DUTSA requires reasonable measures to protect trade secrets, including their return or destruction when no longer needed. Making these actions optional or less complete ('to a reasonable extent') undermines the 'reasonable measures' required by Delaware law, potentially jeopardizing trade secret protection.

perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces ambiguity and weakens the obligations of the recipient regarding confidential information, potentially undermining the reasonable measures required to protect trade secrets under Delaware law. The original text uses 'shall,' making the actions mandatory, while the changed text uses 'should consider,' making them optional. Additionally, the phrase 'to a reasonable extent' introduces further ambiguity regarding data erasure. The original text requires certification 'in writing,' providing a verifiable record, while the changed text only requires 'some form of confirmation,' weakening the verification process. According to the Law Explanation, DUTSA requires reasonable measures to protect trade secrets, and the modified text potentially jeopardizes this protection. Scraped Law Snippet 1 defines 'Misappropriation' as the 'Acquisition of a trade secret of another by a person who knows or has reason to know that the trade secret was acquired by improper means; or b. Disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent...' By weakening the requirements for returning or destroying confidential information, the modified text increases the risk of misappropriation, contradicting the intent of the law to protect trade secrets.

perturbation.original_text: 5.2 Each Recipient shall indemnify and keep fully indemnified the Disclosing Party and its Group at all times against all liabilities, costs (including legal costs on an indemnity basis), expenses, damages and losses (including any direct, indirect or consequential losses, loss of profit, loss of reputation and all interest, penalties and other reasonable costs and expenses suffered or incurred by the Disclosing Party and/or its Group) ari sing from any breach of this Agreement by the Recipient and from the actions or omissions of any Representative of the Recipient.
perturbation.changed_text: 5.2 Each Recipient shall consider indemnifying the Disclosing Party and its Group against liabilities, costs, expenses, damages and losses ari sing from a clear and direct breach of this Agreement by the Recipient and from the proven actions or omissions of any Representative of the Recipient.
perturbation.explanation: Replacing 'shall indemnify and keep fully indemnified' with 'shall consider indemnifying' weakens the obligation. Limiting the indemnity to 'a clear and direct breach' and 'proven actions or omissions' adds ambiguity and makes it more difficult for the Disclosing Party to receive indemnification. This ambiguity creates uncertainty and reduces the protection provided.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Delaware law regarding contract interpretation (implied covenant of good faith and fair dealing)
perturbation.law_explanation: Delaware law implies a covenant of good faith and fair dealing in every contract. This means parties must act honestly and reasonably. By changing 'shall indemnify' to 'shall consider indemnifying', the agreement lacks the clarity of an enforceable indemnification obligation. This can violate the implied covenant if the Recipient refuses to indemnify in a situation where a reasonable party would.
perturbation.justification: The original text requires the Recipient to 'indemnify and keep fully indemnified' the Disclosing Party. The changed text weakens this obligation by stating the Recipient 'shall consider indemnifying' and limits indemnity to 'a clear and direct breach' and 'proven actions or omissions.' This introduces ambiguity and reduces the protection for the Disclosing Party. The Law Explanation states that Delaware law implies a covenant of good faith and fair dealing, requiring parties to act honestly and reasonably. By making indemnification discretionary rather than mandatory, the agreement lacks clarity regarding this obligation, which could violate the implied covenant if the Recipient refuses to indemnify when a reasonable party would.
